title: CSE 312 Official 40-Point Mock Midterm
description: Structured from the shared midterm breakdown, with the multiple-choice section restricted to the TopHat-style question bank.

questions.q: In which of the following situations does buffering NOT apply?
options: When receiving large WebSocket frames
When receiving requests containing large file uploads
When receiving multiple WebSocket frames back-to-back
When receiving a GET request
When a user decides to register with a username that is 1,000,000 characters long

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT true about WebSocket frames?
options: The payload can be masked using XOR
They have a maximum payload size on the order of exabytes
They have a fixed length
They require bit-level parsing to read
The payload will be masked when sent from the client, but not when sent from the server
A new connection using UDP for a WebSocket connection is created
The server understands the WebSocket protocol
A connection to reliably communicate over an unreliable network is established
A request-response connection is established
Nothing, since WebSocket connections are created automatically

questions.q: It is guaranteed that you will always read the entire HTTP request after reading from the TCP socket a single time.
options: False
True

questions.q: Which is NOT a way to process media in this class?
options: Using ffmpeg with system calls
Using ffmpeg with an internal Python library
Using ffmpeg with Python bindings via an external library

questions.q: What is the most reliable way to determine the type of a file?
options: Check the file extension
Assume the type of the file based on the path used to upload the file
All files are just bytes so the type never matters
Read the first bytes of the file to check the file signature
Check the Content-Type header

questions.q: What does Jesse recommend you do while writing your server code?
options: Resign from CSE312
I am not taking this course seriously. Please just give me an F so I can move on with my life.
Do not write the method
Write tests and use the debugger if your tests do not pass
Pick option d because there is no fifth real answer

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT true about multipart/form-data HTTP requests?
options: We receive requests of this type when an HTML form is submitted with enctype multipart/form-data
This format is required if we want to upload files via HTML form submissions
The entire request can always be decoded and parsed as a UTF-8 string
Each part is separated by a boundary found in the Content-Type header
Each form input is contained in a different part of the body with its own headers

questions.q: Why do we use cookies in web apps? Explain the limitation of HTTP that makes cookies necessary.
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] Explicitly state that HTTP is stateless, so each request is handled independently.
[1 pt] State that cookies give the browser/server a way to preserve or re-send state across requests.
[1 pt] Give a concrete example such as authentication, remembering a session, or counting visits.

questions.q: Explain the difference between the `Set-Cookie` header and the `Cookie` header.
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] `Set-Cookie` is sent by the server in a response to tell the browser to store a cookie.
[1 pt] `Cookie` is sent by the browser in later requests to return previously stored cookies.
[1 pt] Mention the one-cookie-per-`Set-Cookie` rule or the fact that the browser combines multiple cookies inside a single `Cookie` header.

questions.q: Differentiate between session cookies and persistent cookies.
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1.5 pts] Session cookies are deleted when the browser session ends and do not have `Expires` or `Max-Age`.
[1.5 pts] Persistent cookies survive browser restarts and are controlled by `Expires` or `Max-Age`.

questions.q: How do the `HttpOnly` and `Secure` flags improve cookie security?
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1.5 pts] `HttpOnly` prevents JavaScript from reading the cookie, which helps defend against session theft through XSS.
[1.5 pts] `Secure` restricts the cookie to HTTPS requests, reducing exposure over insecure transport.

questions.q: Write out a fully formatted HTTP/1.1 POST request from a plain HTML form submitting `username=bob&password=pass123` to `/login` on `localhost:8080`. Include the required headers for the body.
questions.rubric:
2 Points Total:
[1 pt] Correct request line and host header: `POST /login HTTP/1.1` and `Host: localhost:8080`.
[1 pt] Includes `Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded`, `Content-Length: 29`, followed by `\r\n\r\n`, followed by the exact body `username=bob&password=pass123`.

questions.q: Write out a fully formatted HTTP/1.1 response that redirects the browser to `/` and sets a session cookie named `session` equal to `abc123`.
questions.rubric:
2 Points Total:
[1 pt] Correct redirect response line and header: `HTTP/1.1 302 Found` and `Location: /`.
[1 pt] Includes `Set-Cookie: session=abc123`, followed by `\r\n\r\n` (optionally with `Content-Length: 0`).

questions.q: Explain how cookies add state to an otherwise stateless HTTP system. Your answer should include `Set-Cookie`, `Cookie`, session vs persistent cookies, and at least one security concern or directive.
questions.rubric:
4 Points Total:
[1 pt] Explain that HTTP is stateless and cookies are used to attach state across requests.
[1 pt] Mention the flow: the server sends `Set-Cookie`, and the browser later sends `Cookie` back.
[1 pt] Differentiate session cookies from persistent cookies using `Expires` or `Max-Age`.
[1 pt] Include at least one security concern or directive such as `HttpOnly`, `Secure`, XSS, or CSRF.

questions.q: Explain what AJAX / `fetch` gives a web application and how it still fits into the normal HTTP request/response model.
questions.rubric:
4 Points Total:
[1 pt] State that AJAX / `fetch` allows the browser to make HTTP requests after the page has already loaded.
[1 pt] Mention that this can happen without a full page reload, which enables more dynamic interfaces.
[1 pt] Explain that these are still ordinary HTTP requests with methods, headers, and optional bodies.
[1 pt] Give a practical example such as loading chat messages, updating part of a page, or submitting data asynchronously.

questions.q:
Read this Python snippet carefully. There are multiple bugs and vulnerabilities. Find at least 3 issues for full credit, and be ready to separate security problems from ordinary bugs. ```python # Handles an incoming TCP request def handle_request(client_socket): request_data = client_socket.recv(1024).decode() headers, body = request_data.split("\r\n\r\n") username = body.split("=")[1] db.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE user = '" + username + "'") response_body = "

Hello " + username + "

" response = "HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\nContent-Length: " + str(len(response_body)) + "\r\n\r\n" + response_body client_socket.sendall(response.encode()) ```
questions.rubric:
8 Points Total. Grade leniently if the student clearly identifies at least 3 structural flaws, with both security and bug awareness:

Security findings:
1. [Security - SQL Injection] The query concatenates untrusted `username` directly into SQL.
2. [Security - XSS] The response reflects `username` directly into HTML without escaping.
3. [Security - Missing Input Validation] The code trusts form input shape and contents without validation or sanitization.

Bug findings:
4. [Bug - TCP Streaming] A single `recv(1024)` is not guaranteed to contain the full HTTP request.
5. [Bug - Fragile Parsing] `split("\r\n\r\n")` can crash if the delimiter has not fully arrived yet.
6. [Bug - Wrong Content-Length Basis] `len(response_body)` counts Python characters, not UTF-8 bytes.
7. [Bug - Unsafe Decode Assumption] Decoding the whole request before separating headers/body breaks binary uploads and other non-text bodies.

Full credit expectation: finding any 3 strong issues, with at least one security-oriented observation and at least one correctness / parsing bug.
